cards.title: AI's 'Busy Work' Problem: The Hidden Opportunity in Validating, Not Just Generating
cards.thesis: AI models are becoming incredibly cheap and powerful, making it easy to generate vast amounts of content and code. However, this explosion of AI output is creating a new problem: 'busy work' for humans who still need to double-check, validate, and correct the 'slop' (low-quality or inaccurate output), leading to developer anxiety and inefficiency.

evidence.text: Looking at tech blogs, HN submissions, et cetera, a lot of content is (partly, increasingly?) generated by AI. The value of the reader's time and slop has been discussed at length.

When I look at what engineers and non-engineers are doing with LLMs (Claude Code and friends)... I'm finding more and more instances of busywork. Burning tokens is equated with making progress... Only for every single piece of artifact to be double-checked by a human.
DeepSeek-V4-Pro 75% discount promo is now permanent... this is just less than half of Haiku, which is ridiculous?

What to learn and do, that makes me least affected by AI in STEM? ... I lost hope of learning anything new out there to make a reasonable difference and impact. Choose any topic AI knows. Its interpretation is far better than what I can come up with by myself. I feel robbed of my intellectual freedom to express and be acc[ountable].

cards.opportunity: Everyone's feeling the 'busy work' of double-checking AI-generated content and code, especially as models get cheaper and more prolific. Instead of just generating more, the real win is building something that *validates* AI output against a known truth or a specific style guide. Imagine a 'code cop' that doesn't just suggest fixes but automatically checks if AI-generated code meets your project's coding standards or if AI-written docs accurately reflect the current API (Application Programming Interface – a set of rules allowing software to talk to other software). Ship a small tool that hooks into common AI coding assistants like Cursor or Replit and flags non-compliant or outdated AI output *before* it becomes 'busy work' for a human.

cards.title: Stuck on Next.js Basics? There's a Micro-Tool Opportunity Waiting
cards.thesis: Even with great documentation, developers often create personal 'practice' repos for popular frameworks like Next.js, specifically to repeat or solidify core concepts. The presence of multiple, basic Next.js learning repos suggests a persistent need for simple, repeatable ways to grasp fundamental features without diving deep into full courses or complex examples.

evidence.text: A GitHub repository named 'next.js' by zarifboevv13 shows 25 engagements, indicating someone is actively exploring or learning the framework.

Another repository by the same user, 'next.js_takrorlash4' (where 'takrorlash' means 'repetition' in Uzbek), also has 25 engagements, pointing towards a pattern of practicing or repeating Next.js concepts.

cards.opportunity: Everyone's trying to build the next big app with Next.js, but many are still getting tripped up on core patterns, judging by people making 'repetition' repos. You could build a web app that generates tiny, single-feature Next.js projects — like 'a server component with a simple API call' or 'a page with dynamic routing' — giving developers a quick, isolated template for practice. Ship a few of these 'micro-project generators' this weekend, and you'll own the 'just show me how to do THIS one thing' market for Next.js builders.
